summary: Evans describes her delightful experience submitting a pull request to the Rust libc project, where two bots made the process smooth and welcoming. The highfive bot automatically greeted her and assigned a reviewer, while homu (bors) handled test coordination and merging after reviewer approval. She highlights how these bots reduce maintainer burden, especially for repositories with long or flaky test suites. The post is a brief appreciation of how automation can make open source contribution more pleasant for everyone involved.
key_points: The rust-highfive bot automatically welcomes contributors and assigns reviewers, creating a friendly first impression
Homu (bors) automates the test-and-merge workflow so maintainers don't have to babysit CI, especially valuable when tests take an hour or are flaky
Simple automation like greeting bots and merge bots can dramatically improve the open source contribution experience
Homu is the second iteration of bors, originally created by Graydon Hoare for the Rust project
money_quotes: Someone described my approach to blogging as 'fanfiction' recently, a description that I kind of loved.
A lot of the time I write about things that I find in the world that love and my take on them.
Basically he's saying 'this looks reasonable; fine with me as long as the tests pass!'
Homu's job is to make it so that you don't have to keep checking to see if the tests pass!
This is a huge blessing on this particular repository because the tests take like an hour.
key_insight: Simple bots that greet contributors and automate test-and-merge workflows can transform the open source contribution experience from tedious to delightful.
